Ayah 1–7 · linked video: 01. Al-Hashr (Ayah 1-7)

Surah Al-Hashr: Commentary on Ayahs 1-7

Ayah 1

سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

Everything in the heavens and the earth has been declaring the perfection of Allah ﷻ. The ayah begins with this profound reminder that all of creation glorifies Allah ﷻ, establishing His absolute authority before discussing the events that will unfold in this Surah. He ﷻ is Al-Aziz, the Ultimate Authority, and Al-Hakim, the All-Wise. These two names are particularly significant in the context of what follows, as they underscore that the expulsion of Banu Nadir was not merely a political or military event, but a manifestation of divine wisdom and power.

Ayah 2

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَخْرَجَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ لِأَوَّلِ ٱلْحَشْرِ ۚ مَا ظَنَنتُمْ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ ۖ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُم مَّانِعَتُهُمْ حُصُونُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ فَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا۟ ۖ وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ ۚ يُخْرِبُونَ بُيُوتَهُم بِأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَيْدِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ فَٱعْتَبِرُوا۟ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ

This ayah addresses a major event in the Seerah: the expulsion of Banu Nadir, the second of three Jewish tribes in Medina to show treachery against the Prophet ﷺ and the Muslim community. To understand the gravity of this moment, we need to examine the historical context.

By the fifth year after Hijrah, two Jewish tribes had already engaged in acts of treachery against the Muslims. Banu Qaynuqa had been expelled earlier, and now Banu Nadir’s betrayal had been exposed. Among them was Ka’b ibn al-Ashraf, a famous poet who played a crucial role in mobilizing forces against the Muslims. He had promised the Quraysh that they should attack from outside while Banu Nadir would attack from inside Medina. He made secret agreements behind the treaty that existed between the Muslims and the Jews, coordinating with both external enemies and internal hypocrites. On top of this military treachery, he composed poetry insulting the Prophet ﷺ and the believers. When people misunderstand the history, they claim the Prophet ﷺ ordered his execution merely for writing poetry. This is not accurate. His execution was ordered because he was a war criminal, a key player in mobilizing forces and rallying troops against the Muslims at the Battle of Uhud.

When this treachery was fully exposed, Banu Nadir retreated into their fortified stronghold. The Muslims prepared to confront them, but everyone expected a prolonged siege. The Jews had fortifications, weapons, provisions and numbers. They were confident in their defenses.

Allah ﷻ says: هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَخْرَجَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ لِأَوَّلِ ٱلْحَشْرِ - “He is the One who expelled the disbelievers from among the People of the Book from their homes for the first gathering.” The word الْحَشْرِ refers to a gathering or surrounding. The Muslims surrounded the fortress, expecting this to be the first of many attempts to breach their defenses.

مَا ظَنَنتُمْ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ - “You did not think that they would come out.” The Muslims assumed this would take time. They would have to come back again and again, lay siege repeatedly. The fortifications seemed impenetrable.

وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُم مَّانِعَتُهُمْ حُصُونُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ - “And they thought that their fortresses would protect them from Allah.” Notice the brilliance of this expression: مَّانِعَتُهُمْ - “their prevention” - their means of being prevented from attack, their defensive shield. They were confident because of their fortifications, their weaponry, their numbers and their provisions. They felt completely secure.

But then comes the turning point: فَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا۟ - “But Allah came at them from where they had not expected.” This is remarkable. Picture the scene: the Muslims are outside, lacking confidence that they can penetrate the fortress. The Jews are inside, completely confident in their defenses. So from where did Allah ﷻ attack them?

وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ - “And He cast terror into their hearts.” Allah ﷻ launched an attack that only He could launch. He went through the walls of the fortress, through the walls of their chests, and struck directly at their hearts with overwhelming fear. This manic terror gripped them so powerfully that they opened the gates and surrendered. This was an attack of a completely different nature - a divine intervention that bypassed all physical defenses.

يُخْرِبُونَ بُيُوتَهُم بِأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَيْدِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - “They were destroying their homes with their own hands and the hands of the believers.” Part of their humiliation was that when the verdict came for them to leave and take what they could carry, they began tearing down their own homes. They pulled off mantels, lamps, latches from doors, decorations from walls - destroying what they had built with their own hands. The believers even helped them dismantle their homes so they could pack their belongings onto carts and leave.

فَٱعْتَبِرُوا۟ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ - “So take a lesson, O people of vision.” This is directed at those who can truly see. By this point, two of the three Jewish tribes had been expelled for treachery. This is a warning to the third: you better pay attention to what’s happening. Learn from this. The third tribe, Banu Qurayza, would face their reckoning after the Battle of the Trench, as discussed in Surah Al-Ahzab.

Ayah 3

وَلَوْلَآ أَن كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْجَلَآءَ لَعَذَّبَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابُ ٱلنَّارِ

وَلَوْلَآ أَن كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْجَلَآءَ - “And had Allah not decreed expulsion for them.” The word الْجَلَآءَ means to be expelled, to be kicked out of one’s home and land, to become outcasts without the protection of one’s dwelling. It also carries the meaning of being exposed, of coming out into the open where you no longer have shelter. Allah ﷻ ordained that this should be their punishment: expulsion from Medina.

لَعَذَّبَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا - “He would have punished them in this world.” This is crucial to understand: expulsion was actually the easier option. There were many other possibilities that could have befallen them, and those would have been far more severe. Allah ﷻ is making clear that this was an act of mercy compared to what could have been.

وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابُ ٱلنَّارِ - “And for them in the Hereafter is the punishment of the Fire.” Their worldly punishment was lightened, but the consequences in the Akhirah remain.

Ayah 4

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَآقُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۖ وَمَن يُشَآقِّ ٱللَّهَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَآقُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ - “That is because they opposed Allah and His Messenger.” The word شَآقُّوا comes from شِقَاق, which means to be in a schism, to refuse agreement, to take a stance of opposition on every issue. This is different from مُحَادَّة, which we saw in previous surahs. Both express opposition, but shiqaq emphasizes the desire to oppose everything, to take an antagonistic stance regardless of the issue.

Banu Nadir, particularly among the three Jewish tribes, had the sharpest tongues. Even before their expulsion, when the Muslims returned from Uhud, they would mock them: “You think that was tough? You think you fought the Quraysh? One day if you ever fight us, you’ll find out what real fighting means. We’ll show you what battle actually is. Those Quraysh went easy on you.” They constantly taunted the Muslims, saying things like, “You’re lucky we have a treaty.”

وَمَن يُشَآقِّ ٱللَّهَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - “And whoever opposes Allah - then indeed, Allah is severe in penalty.” This is a universal principle: opposing Allah ﷻ and showing animosity toward Him and His Messenger ﷺ brings severe consequences.

Ayah 5

مَا قَطَعْتُم مِّن لِّينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوهَا قَآئِمَةً عَلَىٰٓ أُصُولِهَا فَبِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيُخْزِىَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

This ayah addresses a specific incident during the siege. There were tall palm trees near the fortress, and cutting some of them provided a strategic military advantage - it would expose the fort and remove the enemy’s cover. The Prophet ﷺ commanded that certain trees be cut down for this tactical purpose.

Immediately, the Jews began shouting from inside the fortress: “What kind of prophet is this? He cuts trees! Look at this righteous prophet, destroying perfectly good trees!” This created doubt among some Muslims: cutting down fruit-bearing trees does seem wrong. What is Allah’s verdict on this?

مَا قَطَعْتُم مِّن لِّينَةٍ - “Whatever you cut down of palm trees.” Notice Allah ﷻ doesn’t say شَجَرَة (tree), He says لِّينَةٍ. The word لِّينَةٍ specifically refers to a tree that produces لِين - soft, high-quality dates. These weren’t just any trees; they were valuable agricultural assets producing premium dates.

أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوهَا قَآئِمَةً عَلَىٰٓ أُصُولِهَا - “Or left them standing on their roots.” The Muslims didn’t cut all the trees, only the ones that were strategically necessary.

فَبِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ - “It was by permission of Allah.” This is profound. When the Prophet ﷺ gave this instruction, he didn’t wait for revelation to come down. He made a strategic military decision in the moment. Yet Allah ﷻ confirms that this decision carried divine sanction.

This teaches us something subtle about revelation. Between Surah Al-Mujadilah and Surah Al-Hashr, we learn different dimensions of how revelation works. In Al-Mujadilah, a woman came with a question and the Prophet ﷺ remained completely silent, saying nothing until the ayat came down. But here, when it came to a military decision about cutting trees, did he wait for Quran to come? No. He made the decision.

When should he stay silent and wait for revelation? When should he give a verdict based on the guidance already in his heart? That itself is part of revelation. That discernment - knowing when to wait and when to act - is also وَحْي, divine inspiration.

وَلِيُخْزِىَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ - “And so He would disgrace the defiantly disobedient.” The Jews tried to use this incident to humiliate the Prophet ﷺ and cast doubt among the believers. But Allah ﷻ says this was actually done to humiliate them - the corrupt, the transgressors who had violated every covenant.

Ayah 6

وَمَآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْهُمْ فَمَآ أَوْجَفْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ خَيْلٍ وَلَا رِكَابٍ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَلِّطُ رُسُلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

To understand this ayah, we need to understand the concept of فَيْء. The word comes from the root ف-ي-ء, which means “what was left behind” or “what came back.” In military context, when Muslims go to war and the enemy leaves behind shields, swords, provisions - all of that is called فَيْء, the spoils or property that returns.

Before we continue, we must understand the policy on war spoils that was established earlier in Surah Al-Anfal. Before Islam, the policy was simple: whatever you find on the battlefield is yours. After the battle ends, if you picked up ten shields and five swords, saw a goat and grabbed it - it was all yours.

But Islam changed this. The Prophet ﷺ organized the Muslim army with different positions: some stand guard, some meet the enemy in front lines, some are stationed in different locations. When the battle ends, are all these positions equally profitable? No. The guard who was stationed at his post didn’t get to run around grabbing everything because he was fulfilling his duty at his position.

This caused complaints among the Sahaba (RA). “I didn’t get anything, that’s not fair! I was positioned over here while the guy on the front line got everything!” Arguments broke out among them. Imagine: you went to the store, bought a huge bag of toys, and walked into your house. What happens with your kids? “That’s mine!” “No, that’s mine!” Everyone’s fighting over the toys. The parent gets angry and says, “Okay, nobody gets anything!”

That’s essentially what happened in Surah Al-Anfal. The Sahaba (RA) were asking about the distribution of spoils, and Allah ﷻ revealed: يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَنفَالِ ۖ قُلِ ٱلْأَنفَالُ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ - “They ask you about the spoils of war. Say: The spoils belong to Allah and the Messenger.” Stop fighting. It all belongs to Allah and His Messenger ﷺ.

Then Allah ﷻ scolded the believers for quarreling and eventually revealed a policy: one-fifth goes to the treasury (for the Messenger ﷺ, relatives in need, orphans, the poor, travelers), and the rest can be distributed among the soldiers. Those with horses get twice what those on foot get, because they bear the expense and liability of maintaining their horses.

This established the Sahaba’s understanding: one-fifth to the treasury, the rest distributed among those who fought. But now we come to a second lesson, a similar one that will set their priorities straight again.

وَمَآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْهُمْ - “And whatever Allah restored to His Messenger from them.” This is فَيْء, the spoils that come without a fight, property that returns to the Muslims through a military expedition where there was no actual battle.

فَمَآ أَوْجَفْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ خَيْلٍ وَلَا رِكَابٍ - “You did not spur for it any horses or camels.” You didn’t ride up on the enemy. You didn’t engage in battle. Not a single one of you pulled a sword in this victory.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَلِّطُ رُسُلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ - “But Allah gives His messengers power over whom He wills.” The authoritative victory didn’t come at the hand of any soldier. The credit goes entirely to Allah ﷻ, who defeated the enemy through fear cast into their hearts.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ - “And Allah has power over all things.” He has complete control over everything, including the hearts of His enemies.

Ayah 7

مَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ فَلِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ كَىْ لَا يَكُونَ دُولَةًۢ بَيْنَ ٱلْأَغْنِيَآءِ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ فَٱنتَهُوا۟ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

مَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ - “Whatever Allah restored to His Messenger from the people of the towns.” The Sahaba (RA) were now accustomed to the policy from Surah Al-Anfal: one-fifth to the treasury, the rest distributed among the soldiers.

But now comes a different ruling: فَلِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - “It is for Allah and for the Messenger and for the relatives and orphans and the needy and the traveler in need.”

All of it goes to Allah and His Messenger ﷺ. What does it mean that it belongs to the Messenger ﷺ? It means it goes to where he directs it: to those in need. The close relatives who need support, the orphans, the poor, those stranded in travel - essentially, the welfare of society. Those who require assistance receive it.

But why this policy? Here comes the macroeconomic philosophy of Islam:

كَىْ لَا يَكُونَ دُولَةًۢ بَيْنَ ٱلْأَغْنِيَآءِ مِنكُمْ - “So that it will not be a perpetual distribution among the rich from among you.”

If Islam has a philosophy of economics, this is it. The wealth should not only circulate among the wealthy. This is the problem with certain economic theories that claim if you make the wealthy wealthier, eventually some benefit will “trickle down” to the poor. The idea is that wealthy people will invest more, employ more, and therefore the economy will benefit.

But human history speaks against this concept. When the wealthy get wealthier, what actually tends to happen? The poor get poorer. This isn’t just economic theory that can be proven or disproven - it’s practical, observable reality throughout history.

Islam’s economic principle is different: wealth must circulate throughout society, not concentrate at the top. This is why these spoils - which came without anyone’s individual military effort - go directly to social welfare, to ensure that those without means are taken care of.

وَمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ فَٱنتَهُوا۟ - “And whatever the Messenger has given you - take it; and what he has forbidden you - refrain from it.”

This is one of the most important principles in Islam. The authority of the Prophet ﷺ extends beyond just conveying the Quran. When he gives you something, accept it. When he forbids you from something, stop. This encompasses his Sunnah, his teachings, his guidance, his decisions - like the decision to cut the trees, or the distribution of wealth. His judgment carries divine authority.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - “And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is severe in penalty.”

The Surah began with a reminder of Allah’s wisdom and authority, and these ayahs conclude with the same reminder. Be conscious of Allah ﷻ in how you handle wealth, in how you respond to the Messenger’s ﷺ guidance, and in how you structure your society. The consequences of ignoring these principles are severe, both in this world and the next.

Ayah 7–16 · linked video: 02. Al-Hashr (Ayah 7-16)

Surah Al-Hashr: Ayahs 7-16

Ayah 7

مَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ فَلِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ كَىْ لَا يَكُونَ دُولَةًۢ بَيْنَ ٱلْأَغْنِيَآءِ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ فَٱنتَهُوا۟ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

This ayah establishes a revolutionary economic principle that would shape Islamic civilization for centuries to come. Allah ﷻ declares that whatever spoils come from the people of the towns belong to Him and His Messenger ﷺ, to be distributed among specific categories: relatives, orphans, the poor, and travelers in need.

The phrase كَىْ لَا يَكُونَ دُولَةًۢ بَيْنَ ٱلْأَغْنِيَآءِ مِنكُمْ (so that it does not become wealth circulating among only the rich) contains profound wisdom. Shah Waliullah (RA), one of Islam’s great scholars of the 16th and 17th centuries, demonstrated remarkable foresight regarding this principle. As the modern world was beginning to take shape, he observed that two economic extremes would emerge: obscene wealth concentrated in the hands of a few, and devastating poverty afflicting the masses.

He explained that extreme poverty strips people of their human dignity, reducing them to mere survival. When people live hand-to-mouth, breaking their backs just to feed themselves that day, their connection to faith becomes strained. The hadith warns that poverty can almost lead to disbelief—not because poverty itself destroys faith, but because at a social level, when entire communities are consumed by daily survival, spiritual concerns become distant. You cannot easily discuss the concerns of Paradise and the Hereafter with someone who doesn’t know where their next meal will come from.

On the opposite extreme, obscene wealth creates its own spiritual catastrophe. When people accumulate wealth beyond measure, not knowing what to do with it, they engage in decadence, corruption, and shameless extravagance. This creates a culture of extreme sin at the top of society.

Moreover, these two extremes breed mutual contempt. The wealthy look down upon the poor, barely seeing them as human. The poor look at the rich and ask, “Why do they have everything while we have nothing?” This class warfare tears apart the fabric of society.

Now consider the context: the companions who rode their horses into battle against Banu Nadir naturally had more resources than those who came on foot. They didn’t have to engage in actual combat—the enemy surrendered before fighting began. Should these wealthier companions become even richer from the spoils? Allah ﷻ says no. This wealth should be distributed to the poor, preventing the concentration of resources among those who already have.

This principle shaped Muslim foreign policy in profound ways. When Muslims began conquering territories in Rome and Persia during the time of Umar (RA), some companions argued that according to traditional rules of war, one-fifth should go to the Muslim treasury and eighty percent should be divided among the soldiers who fought. Had this been implemented, it would have created one of history’s greatest aristocracies—tens of thousands of soldiers dividing entire countries among themselves.

Umar (RA) had the wisdom to distinguish between battlefield spoils and conquered lands. Whatever soldiers found on the actual battlefield was theirs to keep, but all conquered land became fai’—spoils returned to Allah ﷻ—and belonged to the Muslim treasury. For the first time in human history, this established the concept of publicly owned land. The revenues generated from these farmlands would be redistributed to benefit the masses, particularly the poor Muslims, rather than enriching a military elite.

This prevented the establishment of an aristocratic class that would own vast estates worked by a subjugated labor force—a system that perpetuates economic slavery. In the modern world, when nations are conquered, corporations typically rush in to buy land and extract resources. The wealth flows upward to further enrich the already wealthy. Islam embedded a completely different paradigm into its legal code.

Even inheritance laws reflect this principle. When a wealthy person dies, their estate must be divided among multiple heirs according to fixed shares—sons, daughters, brothers, sisters, spouses. Daughters who marry take their inheritance into other families. Wealth cannot remain concentrated within a single family line for generations. The system of zakat further ensures that aristocratic dynasties of billionaires cannot emerge. You can be comfortable and well-off, but you will not become obscenely wealthy at the expense of society.

Interestingly, the world’s most struggling economies often have the richest individuals. A handful of people control vast wealth while everyone else can barely survive. This is precisely what Islamic economic principles were designed to prevent.

The ayah continues: وَمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ فَٱنتَهُوا۟ (Whatever the Messenger gives you, take it, and whatever he forbids you from, abstain from it). This wasn’t just about spoils of war—it established a broader principle of following the Messenger’s ﷺ guidance. The companions were human beings with natural expectations. When going into battle, there’s an expectation of gaining something. Banu Nadir was known to be well-equipped with goods, armor, and resources. When the policy came that most soldiers would receive nothing, it required immediate obedience to the prophetic command, even when it contradicted their expectations.

The ayah concludes: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (Be conscious of Allah; indeed, Allah is severe in retribution). This reminder of divine accountability underscores that these aren’t mere suggestions—they are commands backed by the authority of Allah ﷻ Himself.

Ayah 8

لِلْفُقَرَآءِ ٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا وَيَنصُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقُونَ

Having established the distribution policy, Allah ﷻ now identifies who specifically deserves special consideration from these resources: the poor among the Muhajirin (emigrants from Makkah). This designation carries profound significance beyond mere poverty.

The Muhajirin were described as ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ—those expelled from their homes and their wealth. They didn’t just leave Makkah; they were forced out. They lost everything—their houses, their businesses, their assets, their generational wealth. They arrived in Madinah with nothing but their faith.

But notice Allah’s ﷻ emphasis on their motivation: يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا (seeking bounty from Allah and His pleasure). They weren’t refugees fleeing hardship hoping for better economic opportunities. They were believers who sacrificed everything material because they sought something higher—Allah’s pleasure. Their migration wasn’t driven by worldly aspirations but by spiritual commitment.

وَيَنصُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ (and they support Allah and His Messenger). Even after losing everything, they continued to stand firm in supporting the faith and the Prophet ﷺ. They didn’t become bitter or resentful. They didn’t demand compensation for their losses. They simply continued serving the mission of Islam.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقُونَ (Those are the truly truthful ones). This declaration of their truthfulness is significant. Many people claim to have faith, but the Muhajirin proved their truthfulness through sacrifice. Their iman wasn’t just words—it was demonstrated through the most expensive currency: giving up home, wealth, and security for Allah’s sake. When someone leaves behind everything for their faith, their truthfulness is beyond question.

This ayah provides divine justification for why the Muhajirin receive special consideration in the distribution. It’s not simply because they’re poor, and it’s not simply because they’re immigrants—it’s because they’ve already given more than anyone else. They’ve paid a price that few are ever asked to pay.

Ayah 9

وَٱلَّذِينَ تَبَوَّءُو ٱلدَّارَ وَٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ يُحِبُونَ مَنْ هَاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ فِى صُدُورِهِمْ حَاجَةً مِّمَّآ أُوتُوا۟ وَيُؤْثِرُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَوْ كَانَ بِهِمْ خَصَاصَةٌ ۚ وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

After praising the Muhajirin, Allah ﷻ turns His attention to the Ansar (the Helpers of Madinah), ensuring they don’t feel overlooked. This ayah serves as a divine tribute to their extraordinary character.

وَٱلَّذِينَ تَبَوَّءُو ٱلدَّارَ وَٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ (Those who were settled in the home and in faith before them). The Ansar had already established themselves in Madinah, and they had already embraced iman before the Prophet ﷺ arrived, through the efforts of Mus’ab ibn Umair (RA) and other ambassadors of Islam. They were rooted in both place and faith.

يُحِبُونَ مَنْ هَاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ (They love those who emigrated to them). This is remarkable. Which nation can honestly say they love immigrants? Throughout history and across the modern world, immigration typically breeds resentment. When immigrants arrive, host populations commonly complain: “They’re going to drain our economy. They’ll take our jobs. They’ll take our homes. They’ll corrupt our language and culture. We don’t want them here—go back to your country.”

But the Ansar’s situation was even more challenging. These weren’t wealthy refugees with resources. The Muhajirin arrived completely bankrupt, needing full financial support—essentially all on welfare. And Madinah wasn’t an affluent economy. The Ansar themselves were barely making ends meet. They were being asked to share their meager resources, sometimes splitting two meals to feed additional people. Yet Allah ﷻ testifies that they loved the immigrants.

وَلَا يَجِدُونَ فِى صُدُورِهِمْ حَاجَةً مِّمَّآ أُوتُوا۟ (And they find no discomfort in their chests from what they were given). When the previous ayah announced that the Muhajirin would receive special allocation from the spoils of Banu Nadir, natural human feelings might have stirred in the Ansar’s hearts: “Wait—these people already live off our support, and now they get extra bonuses on top of that?” But Allah ﷻ declares they felt no such discomfort. Their hearts were completely at peace with this decision.

This stands in stark contrast to the munafiqun (hypocrites), who would certainly have felt and expressed such resentment. The hypocrites, who hated battle and showed up reluctantly, would have been furious to receive nothing while others received rewards.

وَيُؤْثِرُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ (And they give preference to others over themselves). The word يُؤْثِرُونَ comes from آثَرَ , meaning to give preference, to prioritize someone else above yourself. This wasn’t simply charity or giving from surplus—it was self-sacrifice. They put others’ needs before their own.

وَلَوْ كَانَ بِهِمْ خَصَاصَةٌ (even if they themselves were in dire need). The word خَصَاصَة derives from roots meaning extreme poverty, destitution—the state of someone who is homeless and has nothing to eat. Allah ﷻ says the Ansar gave preference to others over themselves even when they themselves were hungry, even when they themselves had nothing.

This level of generosity transcends normal human behavior. It represents a spiritual elevation where material attachment has been subdued by something higher.

Then comes the universal principle: وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (And whoever is protected from the stinginess of their own soul—those are the successful ones).

The word شُحَّ refers to the internal greed, the miserliness, the desire for more that is encoded into our very nature. This ayah acknowledges that all of us have some level of greed built into our personalities. It’s part of being human. When you bring home something delicious and children appear, you want to secure your portion first—that’s natural human greed manifesting even in small ways.

Some level of self-interest is actually necessary for survival. But when you’re part of a community, when brotherhood has been established, that greed must be kept in check. The passive verb يُوقَ (he is protected) indicates that overcoming this greed requires divine assistance. We cannot conquer it purely through willpower; we need Allah’s help to be protected from our own internal stinginess.

فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (Those are the ones who have truly attained success). Real success isn’t measured in wealth accumulated or status achieved—it’s measured in the ability to fight the animal nature within ourselves. Those who can transcend their natural greed and give to others despite their own need have achieved something genuinely extraordinary.

Ayah 10

وَٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَلِإِخْوَٰنِنَا ٱلَّذِينَ سَبَقُونَا بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ فِى قُلُوبِنَا غِلًّا لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Having honored the Muhajirin and the Ansar, Allah ﷻ now addresses everyone who comes after them—all subsequent generations of believers, including us.

وَٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ (And those who came after them). This encompasses every Muslim who accepts Islam after that first blessed generation. We are included in this description.

يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَلِإِخْوَٰنِنَا ٱلَّذِينَ سَبَقُونَا بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ (They say: Our Lord, forgive us and our brothers who preceded us in faith). Notice how this ayah teaches us to make dua. We should ask forgiveness not only for ourselves but for those who came before us in faith—the Sahaba (RA), the Tabi’een, all the righteous believers of previous generations. They are our brothers and sisters in faith, connected to us across centuries through the bond of iman.

وَلَا تَجْعَلْ فِى قُلُوبِنَا غِلًّا لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (And do not place in our hearts any ill feeling toward those who believe). The word غِلًّا refers to resentment, jealousy, ill will, or grudges. It includes the feeling of “Why do they have what I don’t have?” It encompasses worldly aspirations that make us covetous of what others possess.

Allah ﷻ uses the word غِلّ in reference to the Prophet ﷺ elsewhere in the Quran, stating it’s not appropriate for a prophet to have ghul—to have worldly aspirations or to compete for what others have. We’re being taught to pray for protection from these feelings.

This prayer is particularly significant in context. The previous ayahs just described how the Muhajirin received special allocation while the Ansar received praise but no material reward. A later generation might think, “Why did they get that and we didn’t? Why were we born later?” This dua prevents such feelings from taking root.

We’re being taught not to be jealous of other believers, not to resent the blessings Allah ﷻ has given them, not to question why some people have what we don’t. Instead, we should be genuinely happy for what our brothers and sisters receive.

رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Our Lord, indeed You are the Most Kind, the Most Merciful). The dua concludes by appealing to Allah’s attributes of gentleness (رَءُوفٌ) and mercy (رَّحِيمٌ). We acknowledge that He is compassionate toward our weaknesses and merciful in dealing with our faults. We need His kindness to protect our hearts from destructive emotions.

Ayah 11

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ نَافَقُوا۟ يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَلَا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَدًا أَبَدًا وَإِن قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنصُرَنَّكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ

The surah now shifts dramatically to expose the treachery of the hypocrites. After describing the nobility of true believers, Allah ﷻ reveals the pathetic reality of those who pretend to believe.

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ نَافَقُوا۟ (Have you not seen those who engage in hypocrisy?). The rhetorical question draws our attention to observe their behavior carefully.

يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (They say to their brothers who have disbelieved from the People of the Book). Allah ﷻ calls the Jewish tribes “their brothers”—not because of faith, but because they share the same treacherous character. The hypocrites had been holding secret meetings with the Jewish tribes who were violating their treaties with the Muslims.

لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ (If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you). The hypocrites were making promises: “We’re with you. If the Muslims kick you out of Madinah, we’ll leave with you.”

وَلَا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَدًا أَبَدًا (And we will never obey anyone against you). They were essentially saying, “We might appear Muslim on the outside, but we’re never going to follow any policy that requires us to fight you. We don’t care who gives that order—even if it comes from Muhammad ﷺ himself—we won’t follow it. You’re our allies.”

وَإِن قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنصُرَنَّكُمْ (And if you are fought, we will certainly help you). “We’ve picked our side. We’re with you. If Muslims fight you, we’ll support you.”

These were serious declarations of betrayal—secret promises made to the enemies of Islam while pretending to be part of the Muslim community.

وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (And Allah testifies that they are certainly liars). Allah ﷻ Himself bears witness that these promises are completely false. The hypocrites were trying to impress both sides—buttering up the Muslims on one hand while assuring the Jews on the other—trying to ensure they remain safe regardless of which side wins.

One defining characteristic of the munafiq is the obsession with safety and the absolute hatred of the battlefield. Whether the battle is alongside the Muslims or against them, the hypocrite will not participate. He’s full of empty talk, making promises he has no intention of keeping, trying to secure his position through deception.

Ayah 12

لَئِنْ أُخْرِجُوا۟ لَا يَخْرُجُونَ مَعَهُمْ وَلَئِن قُوتِلُوا۟ لَا يَنصُرُونَهُمْ وَلَئِن نَّصَرُوهُمْ لَيُوَلُّنَّ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ

Allah ﷻ now exposes what will actually happen, revealing the reality behind the hypocrites’ false promises.

لَئِنْ أُخْرِجُوا۟ لَا يَخْرُجُونَ مَعَهُمْ (If they are expelled, they will not leave with them). Despite their promises of solidarity, when the Jewish tribes are actually expelled, the hypocrites will stay safely behind in Madinah.

وَلَئِن قُوتِلُوا۟ لَا يَنصُرُونَهُمْ (And if they are fought, they will not help them). When battle comes, despite all their assurances of support, the hypocrites will not show up to fight alongside their “brothers.”

وَلَئِن نَّصَرُوهُمْ لَيُوَلُّنَّ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ (And even if they did help them, they would certainly turn their backs). Allah ﷻ goes further: even in the hypothetical scenario where they somehow did show up to help, they would absolutely flee and abandon their allies.

ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (Then they would not be helped). In the end, neither the Jewish tribes nor the hypocrites would receive any support. Their alliance is worthless because it’s built on lies and cowardice.

Ayah 13

لَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ رَهْبَةً فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ

This ayah reveals a profound psychological truth about the hypocrites and their Jewish allies.

لَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ رَهْبَةً فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ (You are certainly more fearsome in their hearts than Allah). The word رَهْبَةً means fear, terror, or dread. Allah ﷻ declares that in the depths of their hearts, they fear the Muslims more than they fear Him.

Think about what this means. They don’t feel genuine fear of Allah ﷻ—His punishment, His accountability, His displeasure. What they truly fear is social exposure. They’re terrified that Muslims might discover they’re actually hypocrites. Their reputations are more precious to them than their standing before Allah ﷻ. Social consequences are more real to them than divine consequences.

This reveals the fundamental problem with hypocrisy: it’s rooted in fearing people more than fearing Allah ﷻ. The hypocrite’s calculations are entirely worldly—he’s concerned about his image, his position, his safety among people—while remaining heedless of his standing before the Creator.

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (That is because they are a people who do not understand). The word يَفْقَهُونَ comes from فِقْه —deep understanding, comprehension, insight. They lack fundamental understanding. If they truly understood reality—if they grasped who Allah ﷻ is, what the Hereafter means, what the consequences of their actions will be—they would never behave this way. Their problem isn’t lack of information; it’s lack of genuine comprehension.

Ayah 14

لَا يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ جَمِيعًا إِلَّا فِى قُرًى مُّحَصَّنَةٍ أَوْ مِن وَرَآءِ جُدُرٍۭ ۚ بَأْسُهُم بَيْنَهُمْ شَدِيدٌ ۚ تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعًا وَقُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّىٰ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ

Allah ﷻ continues exposing the military weakness and internal divisions of these enemies.

لَا يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ جَمِيعًا إِلَّا فِى قُرًى مُّحَصَّنَةٍ أَوْ مِن وَرَآءِ جُدُرٍۭ (They will not fight you all together except in fortified towns or from behind walls). They will never meet you in open battle, face to face on the battlefield as was traditional in warfare. Classical battles involved two armies gathering and clashing directly. But these enemies will only fight from positions of strategic advantage—from within fortified settlements or from behind protective barriers. They won’t risk direct confrontation.

بَأْسُهُم بَيْنَهُمْ شَدِيدٌ (Their violence among themselves is severe). Here’s inside information about the Jewish community of Madinah: their internal conflicts and wars among themselves are intense. They fight each other viciously.

تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعًا وَقُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّىٰ (You think they are united, but their hearts are divided). From the outside, they appear to present a united front. You might assume they’re all on the same page, working together. But their hearts are شَتَّىٰ —scattered, divided, pulling in different directions. There’s no real unity among them, only the appearance of unity.

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ (That is because they are a people who do not use their intellect). The word يَعْقِلُونَ comes from عَقْل —intellect, reasoning, rational thought. If they truly thought about what serves their best interests, if they applied genuine rational analysis, they would recognize that Islam itself is the best option for them. But they refuse to consider that possibility. They’re capable of tactical cleverness but lack true wisdom.

Ayah 15

كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ قَرِيبًا ۖ ذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

This ayah draws a comparison to teach through historical example.

كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ قَرِيبًا (Like the example of those shortly before them). They’re just like people who came before them in the recent past—not ancient history, but recent events that everyone still remembers.

This refers to the disbelievers at the Battle of Badr. The Quraysh came out with confidence, with superior numbers and equipment, expecting an easy victory. Instead, they suffered a devastating defeat.

ذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ (They tasted the consequence of their affair). The word وَبَالَ refers to severe consequences, harmful results, devastating outcomes. At Badr, they directly experienced the disastrous results of their decisions—their arrogance, their opposition to the truth, their confidence in their own strength over Allah’s power.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (And for them is painful punishment). Beyond the worldly defeat they already tasted, they face painful punishment in the Hereafter. The consequences aren’t limited to this world—they extend into eternity.

The message to the Jewish tribes and hypocrites is clear: you’re following the same path as those who already suffered devastating defeat. Learn from their example before you share their fate.

Ayah 16

كَمَثَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ إِذْ قَالَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ ٱكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

The surah concludes this section with a powerful parable that encapsulates the entire dynamic between the hypocrites and the Jewish tribes.

كَمَثَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ (Like the example of Shaytan). Their relationship mirrors the archetypal relationship between Shaytan and human beings.

إِذْ قَالَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ ٱكْفُرْ (When he says to the human being: Disbelieve). Shaytan whispers, encourages, and entices people toward disbelief. He makes promises, offers assurances, presents disbelief as advantageous or liberating.

فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ (Then when he disbelieved). Once the person actually commits to disbelief, once they’ve taken that step based on Shaytan’s encouragement—

قَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكَ (He says: Indeed, I am disassociated from you). Shaytan immediately abandons them, declaring himself free of any responsibility. “I have nothing to do with you. You’re on your own.”

إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (Indeed, I fear Allah, Lord of the worlds). In a final act of hypocrisy, Shaytan even claims to fear Allah ﷻ—the very Allah ﷻ he spent all his time encouraging others to disobey.

This is exactly the dynamic between the hypocrites and the Jewish tribes. The hypocrites encouraged the Jews: “Don’t worry, we’re with you. If you resist, we’ll support you. If you’re expelled, we’ll leave with you.” They enticed them into confrontation with the Muslims. But when the actual consequences came, the hypocrites abandoned them completely, leaving them to face the results alone.

This parable also serves as a universal warning about the nature of evil companionship. Anyone who encourages you toward sin, who makes disobedience seem attractive, who promises to stand by you in wrongdoing—that person will abandon you when consequences arrive. You’ll face the punishment alone while they distance themselves from you.

The image of Shaytan claiming “I fear Allah” at the moment of betrayal is particularly striking. It reveals the depths of deception and hypocrisy—using even the language of piety as a tool of abandonment.

Ayah 16–24 · linked video: 03. Al-Hashr (Ayah 16-24)

Surah Al-Hashr: Ayahs 16-24

Ayah 16

كَمَثَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ إِذْ قَالَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ ٱكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

This ayah presents a powerful comparison: the hypocrites are like Shaytan in their treachery. Shaytan approaches the human being with promises of support, saying “Just disbelieve, I’ve got you covered.” But the moment that person falls into disbelief, Shaytan immediately abandons them, declaring “إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكَ” - I have nothing to do with you, I am completely absolved from you, I have no association with you whatsoever.

The irony deepens when Shaytan claims “إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ” - I fear Allah ﷻ, the Master of all nations. This is the ultimate betrayal: the one who led you to disbelief now claims to fear Allah ﷻ and distances himself from you completely.

This pattern mirrors exactly what the hypocrites were doing with the Jewish tribes. They approached them saying “We’ve got your back, we’ll support you,” but when the moment of truth arrived, they abandoned them entirely, just as Shaytan does. The comparison reveals the fundamental nature of hypocrisy - false promises followed by complete abandonment when it matters most.

Ayah 17

فَكَانَ عَـٰقِبَتَهُمَآ أَنَّهُمَا فِى ٱلنَّارِ خَـٰلِدَيْنِ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

The consequence of this deception is devastating: both Shaytan and those who listened to him end up in the Fire together. Some scholars interpret this as referring specifically to Shaytan and his follower, while others see it encompassing all those who engage in this pattern of deception and betrayal. They will remain in the Fire forever - خَـٰلِدَيْنِ فِيهَا - and this is the just compensation for those who wrong themselves and others.

Ayah 18

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

After warning against the pattern of Shaytan’s betrayal, Allah ﷻ addresses the believers directly with one of the most beautiful passages in the Quran regarding the protection of iman. The command begins: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - those of you who have iman, be cautious of Allah ﷻ.

Then comes a profound instruction: وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ - every person should keep watch. The word نَظَرَ in Arabic means to look, but it implies something deeper than a casual glance. It means to look for a long time, to maintain constant vigilance. Every soul should keep long, constant watch over what? مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ - what it has invested for tomorrow, what it has sent forward for the morrow.

The classical translation says “what the soul sent forth for the morrow,” but this means: what have you invested into your future? Tomorrow here refers to the Day of Judgment. It shows the urgency of that Day - it’s not some distant event, it’s as if it’s happening tomorrow. What have I done today so I can stand before Allah ﷻ tomorrow? Am I watching myself with that level of awareness? That’s real taqwa.

The ayah concludes by repeating the command: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - have taqwa of Allah ﷻ. Why does the Quran say it twice? First, watch yourself - examine what you’re sending forward. Then, have taqwa of Allah ﷻ again. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - Allah ﷻ is in full view and has complete knowledge of everything you do. He sees what you’re preparing for tomorrow.

Ayah 19

وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

This ayah contains one of the most profound psychological insights in the Quran. Allah ﷻ commands: وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - don’t become like those who forgot Allah ﷻ. In the previous surah, Al-Mujadilah, we learned that Shaytan got hold of such people, putting them on autopilot, making them forget the remembrance of Allah ﷻ.

But then comes the devastating consequence: فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ - He made them forget their own selves. This requires deep reflection. What does it mean to forget your own self? It doesn’t mean forgetting your name, your address, your identity, or neglecting to eat and sleep.

We have two dimensions of existence. There’s our animal existence - we eat, we sleep, we drink, we need clothing. That’s what I see of you and you see of me. But there’s another dimension, another element that will live on beyond this body. There’s something living inside me that is actually who I truly am. That’s the real you, the real me - the nafs, the ruh that lives beyond this material existence.

People who remember Allah ﷻ remain connected to that real self living inside them. But people who forget Allah ﷻ - that real self, that soul inside them, becomes forgotten. When that happens, all that remains is focus on the body: eat, sleep, look good, dress nicely, be comfortable, enjoy luxuries, entertain yourself. Everything becomes about physical comfort and pleasure.

This is Allah’s ultimate psychological punishment in this world. When you forget Him, He reduces your consciousness to that of an animal. You forget your real self, and when you can’t remember who you truly are, how can you take care of yourself? How many people live without realizing the real consequence of forgetting Allah ﷻ? They exist like animals - just consuming, fulfilling urges, entertaining themselves until they die.

The human being in this state becomes a pure consumer, leaving only waste and wreckage behind. Whatever this physical existence takes in, it leaves behind as damage. We wear clothes and they get ruined. We eat food and it becomes waste. Everything we consume in this purely physical state comes out as destruction.

The ruh, however, is different. When the ruh consumes good, good comes out. When Allah ﷻ created our bodies, He used soil as the ingredient. But He didn’t command the angels to prostrate to that clay. He said: فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى - “Once I have fashioned him and blown My ruh into him, then prostrate to him.” The human being was honored when? When the ruh entered. There are plenty of creatures made of dirt - that’s nothing special. What makes us honored is the ruh inside us.

This is a profound question for our personal lives: Where am I headed? People have midlife crises at forty, thinking they’re about to die. But your body will die - you will live on. We should have our crisis in youth. When you’re young, when your body is demanding the most from you, that’s when you’re most answerable to Allah ﷻ. That’s when these years are most valuable. This is when you’re most capable of becoming completely oblivious to the needs of your soul.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ - those are the inherently corrupt. The word fisq in Arabic relates to fasad - something going bad, becoming rotten, violated. You violate yourself with this behavior. Allah ﷻ gave you this beautiful ruh, and what do we do? We trap it inside our hearts, wrapping our hearts with a hard surface like rock, made of desires and jealousy. We let that soul suffocate deep inside, not letting it out, not letting it speak. The conscience is killed.

Ayah 20

لَا يَسْتَوِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ

Allah ﷻ declares: لَا يَسْتَوِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ - the people of the Fire and the people of Jannah are not equal. This ayah carries a dual meaning. On one level, it speaks of the obvious reality on the Day of Judgment. But on another level, it addresses our present state: the one who has suffocated his soul is already living in a fire of misery now, while those whose souls remain intact enjoy a contentment that nobody else can appreciate.

When you truly remember Allah ﷻ - when your heart is connected to Him - there’s a peace, a contentment you experience. You begin tasting Jannah in this life. But when you’re completely oblivious to Allah ﷻ in your daily existence, misery has already begun. You live a miserable existence even if outwardly everything seems fine.

أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ - the people of Jannah, they are the ones who have attained ultimate success. This success begins in this world, in the state of your heart right now.

Ayah 21

لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُۥ خَـٰشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ

This ayah is so profound, so heavy in its implication. لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ - had We sent down this Quran on a mountain. Remember when Musa (AS) asked to see Allah ﷻ? Allah ﷻ told him “You cannot see Me,” but to help him understand something of His glory, Allah ﷻ showed a glimpse - just a little bit - of His manifestation to a mountain. What happened? The mountain exploded, and Musa (AS) fell unconscious.

Allah ﷻ sent this Quran not on mountains, but on the heart of a human being. What does the heart contain? The remembrance of Allah ﷻ. If We had sent this Quran on a mountain, لَّرَأَيْتَهُۥ خَـٰشِعًا - you would have seen it turn soft. The word خَشِعًا comes from the same root that describes your muscles relaxing out of fear, becoming numb. You would see this massive mountain starting to melt.

مُّتَصَدِّعًا - imploding, exploding, tearing apart. مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ - from the fear of Allah ﷻ, from the awe of Allah ﷻ. That’s the effect this Quran would have on a mountain.

But here’s the devastating reality: it’s the same Quran we listen to in our cars, the same Quran we hear in lectures. Yet this rock inside our chests - our hearts - is harder than any mountain. Nothing happens. No explosion, no breaking open. When rocks tear open, what comes out? Water. In the Quran, Allah ﷻ describes waterfalls coming from broken rocks. When the rock of the heart breaks open, what should come out? Tears. But how is it that this rock isn’t opening? How is it that the word of Allah ﷻ is being recited and we’re listening, yet there’s no movement?

Allah ﷻ showed His glory to a mountain and it couldn’t take it. In the case of our Messenger ﷺ, that glory is manifested in His word. The word of Allah ﷻ contains that same glory, giving you exposure to it.

The previous discussion was about those who forget Allah ﷻ, and how He makes them forget themselves. This Quran came to remind you of your true self. Allah ﷻ is telling us that no other creation was worthy of receiving this Quran. Your heart was given something special. Your nafs is something extraordinary. It can carry His word and share it with others. Yet how far we are from truly feeling what this Quran is, from appreciating what this Quran really represents.

وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ - And these are the examples We strike for people so they can engage in deep thought. Notice that even though this surah has been addressing believers, Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “for the believers” or “for the Muslims” here. He says لِلنَّاسِ - for the people, for humanity. Why? Because He doesn’t honor with the title of believer those in whose hearts nothing happens when they hear His word. He simply calls them “people,” hoping they will think, hoping they will reflect - لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ.

Ayah 22

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

The surah commanded us: don’t be from those who forget Allah ﷻ, lest He makes you forget yourselves. Now, to protect us from that fate, this surah - like no other - reminds us of Allah ﷻ through His most beautiful names. These closing ayahs contain the highest concentration of Allah’s names in the Quran. Remember Allah ﷻ by these names, and you won’t forget yourself. You won’t violate the rights of your own soul.

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - He is Allah ﷻ, the One besides whom there is no deity worthy of worship and obedience. No one deserves worship and obedience in any way, shape or form except He Himself.

عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ - the Knower of the unseen and the seen. When He says “the unseen and the seen,” it means unseen and seen as we perceive it. To Allah ﷻ, everything is seen. What you cannot see and what you can see - He knows it all.

In the context of this passage, what is the unseen and the seen? The unseen is the state of my heart, and the seen is my outward appearance. The seen is when the hypocrites claimed iman, and the unseen was their inner treachery. Allah ﷻ knows what’s going on deep inside me and what’s outside - what I say and what I truly mean, what I show and what I hide.

هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ - He is Ar-Rahman, Ar-Rahim. He is the Overwhelmingly Merciful, the One whose mercy is unimaginable in its scope. He is the Constantly Merciful, the perpetual source of mercy. Despite all our shortcomings, despite how hard our hearts have become, His mercy continues to flow.

Ayah 23

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ

The declaration continues: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - He is Allah ﷻ, the One besides whom none deserves worship or obedience in any way, shape or form.

ٱلْمَلِكُ - the King, the Sovereign. When we appreciate Allah’s kingship, we realize we are owned by Him completely. Everything we have belongs to Him.

ٱلْقُدُّوسُ - the source of all purity. The word quddus means absolutely pure, free from any defect or imperfection. When we appreciate that Allah ﷻ is Al-Quddus, we realize we will remain dirty, stained by our sins, until He cleanses us. He alone is the One who purifies.

ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ - As-Salam, the source of peace. All peace comes from Him alone. We will never find peace in life until Allah ﷻ gives it to us. In our prayers we say “As-salamu ’alayka ayyuhan-nabiyyu” and “As-salamu ’alayna” - peace be upon the Prophet ﷺ and peace be upon us. We acknowledge: You are peace, and peace comes from You. There is no peace in my life unless You grant it to me.

ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ - the One who grants iman, the One who gives security and faith. He is the source of belief itself.

ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ - the Guardian, the One who watches constantly over someone and ensures they don’t go in the wrong direction. This is the meaning of muhaymin - protective watchfulness, guiding and guarding.

ٱلْعَزِيزُ - the One who possesses ultimate authority and power, the Mighty, the Honorable, the One who cannot be overcome.

ٱلْجَبَّارُ - This name carries profound meaning. Jabara in Arabic is used for setting a broken bone back in place, for forcefully correcting something that’s out of alignment. When a bone is dislocated, someone forces it back into its socket - that’s jabr. Al-Jabbar is the One who will set you straight, forcefully if necessary. He can correct you, redirect you, set you right then and there. When I go astray and do wrong, and Allah ﷻ hasn’t done anything to me yet, hasn’t set me straight yet - that’s His mercy. But He is Al-Jabbar. He has the power to correct me immediately if He chooses.

ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ - the Possessor of greatness, the One who has every right to greatness. When we recognize that Allah ﷻ is Al-Mutakabbir, the only One who truly owns greatness, we realize we have no basis for pride or ego. If there is One who truly possesses kibr (greatness), it’s Him alone. For us to have kibr is to claim something that doesn’t belong to us.

سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ - How far above is Allah ﷻ from all the false allegations they attribute to Him, from all the partners they associate with Him.

Ayah 24

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ - He is Allah ﷻ, Al-Khaliq, the Creator, the One who brings into existence from nothing.

ٱلْبَارِئُ - the Originator, the One who initiates, who begins things from absolute non-existence.

ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ - the Fashioner, the Molder, the Sculptor. This name speaks to how intimately Allah ﷻ designed each of us. People complain: I’m too short, I’m too tall, I’m too this or too that. I don’t like my features, my voice, my appearance. But Allah ﷻ calls Himself Al-Musawwir - the One who sculpted you. He fashioned your nose, sculpted your eyes, formed your face, shaped your features. When you look at yourself in the mirror, you should thank Al-Musawwir who sculpted you exactly as you are.

لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ - To Him alone belong all the most beautiful names. Every attribute of perfection, every quality of excellence belongs to Him.

يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - Everything in the heavens and the earth continues to declare His perfection. Notice how the surah began: سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - whatever is in the heavens and earth glorifies Allah ﷻ. Now at the end: يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ - it continues to glorify Him.

What’s the point of mentioning this at the very end? It’s a powerful reminder: be like the rest of creation. Everything else doesn’t forget Allah ﷻ. Everything else is fulfilling its purpose. What happened to you? Why are you off track? Why are you forgetting your purpose of existence? The entire universe is in harmony, glorifying its Creator - don’t be the discordant note.

وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ - And He is Al-Aziz, Al-Hakim - the Ultimate Authority and the Owner of all wisdom. Everything He does, every decree He makes, every situation He places you in carries perfect wisdom, even when we cannot see it.

May Allah ﷻ not make us of those who have forgotten themselves. May He make us of those who remember Him through His most beautiful names. May He make all of our supplications sincere and genuine, coming truly from the heart.
